questionText: Consider the condition $P:$ a quadrilateral has four equal sides, and the property $Q:$ the quadrilateral is a square. Classify $P$ as necessary, sufficient, both, or neither, as a condition for $Q$, justifying each part of your classification.

steps.description: Name the condition and the property.
steps.workingLatex: P:\ \text{quadrilateral has four equal sides}, \qquad Q:\ \text{it is a square}
steps.explanation: Fixing the labels keeps the two ideas from blurring together as we test them. We are asked how $P$ relates to $Q$, not the reverse.

steps.description: Recall the precise meaning of the two words.
steps.workingLatex: P \text{ sufficient for } Q \iff P \Rightarrow Q; \qquad P \text{ necessary for } Q \iff Q \Rightarrow P
steps.explanation: 'Sufficient' means the condition is enough to guarantee the property, an arrow from $P$ to $Q$. 'Necessary' means the property cannot hold without the condition, an arrow from $Q$ back to $P$.
Thinking of the words as 'enough' and 'required' makes the two directions concrete. We now check each one against the geometry.

steps.description: Test sufficiency by hunting for a counterexample.
steps.workingLatex: \text{Is } P \Rightarrow Q? \ \text{ Seek four equal sides without being a square}
steps.explanation: To check whether four equal sides is enough, we look for a shape that has them yet fails to be a square. One counterexample would settle the sufficiency question.

steps.description: Provide a rhombus as the counterexample.
steps.workingLatex: \text{Rhombus with angles } 60^\circ, 120^\circ:\ \text{four equal sides, not a square}
steps.explanation: A tilted rhombus has all four sides equal but its angles are not right angles, so it is not a square. This exhibits $P$ true while $Q$ is false.

steps.description: Conclude that P is not sufficient.
steps.explanation: Because a shape can satisfy $P$ without satisfying $Q$, the condition is not enough on its own. So $P$ is not sufficient for $Q$.

steps.description: Test necessity by checking Q ⇒ P.
steps.workingLatex: \text{Is } Q \Rightarrow P? \ \text{ Every square has four equal sides}
steps.explanation: Now we ask whether a square must have four equal sides. This follows straight from the definition of a square.
Since being a square forces the four equal sides, the property cannot hold without the condition. So $P$ is necessary for $Q$.

steps.description: Identify what extra condition sufficiency would need.
steps.explanation: A square requires equal angles as well as equal sides, and the rhombus is precisely the case where the angle condition is missing. This shows exactly why $P$ falls short of sufficiency.

steps.workingLatex: P \text{ is necessary but not sufficient for } Q
steps.explanation: The property demands the condition ($Q\Rightarrow P$) but the condition does not deliver the property ($P\not\Rightarrow Q$). Four equal sides is required for a square, yet not enough to make one.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: Having four equal sides is a necessary but not sufficient condition for being a square: every square has four equal sides ($Q\Rightarrow P$), but a non-square rhombus also has four equal sides, so $P\not\Rightarrow Q$.
